# Discovery of novel bioactive natural products driven by genome mining

### Affiliations

# Discovery of novel bioactive natural products driven by genome mining

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

Microbial natural product is an important source for drug discovery. As more and more microbial genomes are sequenced, bioinformatics analysis shows that there are huge resources of novel natural products. Genome mining is a new strategy of natural product discovery based on gene cluster sequences and biosynthetic pathways. At the same time, it can directly associate the structures of natural products with synthetic pathways, and facilitate the study of biosynthesis and combinatorial biosynthesis. In this paper, the strategies of genome mining, including bioinformatics predictions, metabolomic comparisons and genetic manipulations, are reviewed, which shows a great advantage of this strategy in exploiting the potential of microbial natural products. With the development of genome mining methodology and techniques, it will be possible to realize rational exploitation of microbial natural product resources.

**Keywords:**
Genome mining; biosynthesis; gene cluster; microorganisms; natural products.

Natural product databases for drug discovery: Features and applications
=======================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Tao Zeng, Jiahao Li, Ruibo Wu

Adv. 2 (2024) 100050]")

Pharmaceutical Science Advances, Volume 2, December 2024, Pages 100054

Tao Zeng, Jiahao Li, Ruibo Wu

[View PDF](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000205/pdfft?md5=e2b4f1ac9d62a64a9b6e47ee4c4c9bea&pid=1-s2.0-S2773216924000205-main.pdf)

Abstract
--------

Natural products (NPs) exhibit diverse chemical structures and biological activities that make them valuable sources for drug discovery. With advancements in computational technology, computation-enabled natural drug discovery is gaining increasing significance, with NP databases playing a pivotal role. In light of this, we first summarize the key features of NP databases, including structural data, property annotations, biological sources, biosynthetic pathways, and web interfaces. Subsequently, the wide applications of these databases in drug discovery, such as virtual screening, knowledge graph construction, and molecular generation, are reviewed. We further discuss the puzzle of database development, focusing on data quality and updating. Finally, we emphasize the pivotal role of team collaboration and toolkit innovation in harnessing the immense potential of NP-related databases to accelerate bioactivity mining, structure modification, and manufacturing. This review aims to elucidate the key features and applications of NP databases, with the goal of aiding researchers in developing and maintaining high-quality NP databases for drug discovery.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000114)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000187)

1. Introduction
---------------

Natural products (NPs) are substances generated by living organisms and typically exclude large molecules such as proteins and nucleic acids. NPs have long been a significant source for drug discovery because of their diverse chemical structures and biological activities. Over the past four decades (1981–2019), more than half of the small-molecule drugs worldwide have originated directly or indirectly from NPs [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib2)]. Penicillin, an antibiotic derived from microbial sources, has been used to synthesize a wide range of antibiotics. Artemisinin is an antimalarial drug extracted from _Artemisia annua_ [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib3)], and paclitaxel is an anticancer drug derived from _Taxus brevifolia_ [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib4)]. In the last decade (2014–2023), the U.S. FDA has approved 28 small-molecule drugs related to NPs, with nine directly sourced from NPs and 19 derived from the semi-synthesis of NPs [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib5)]. Examples include tauroursodeoxycholic acid [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib6)], which is derived from bear bile for anti-apoptotic purposes, and rezafungin [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib7)], an antifungal drug semi-synthesized from echinocandin isolated from _Aspergillus delacroxii_.

Although natural-product-based drug discovery has immense potential, challenges still exist. First, more powerful tools are required for active compound mining. The COCONUT NP database contains over 400,000 molecules [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib8)], whereas the NPASS database, which catalogs active NPs, includes fewer than 100,000 molecules [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib9)], representing only a quarter of the total number of NPs ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#fig1)A). As not all existing NPs exhibit biological activity, identifying molecules with specific activities or targets and predicting the activities of newly discovered molecules from the vast chemical space remain formidable challenges in the field. The second challenge lies in the fact that despite the positive results of many NPs in activity tests, drug-likeness and off-target effects must be further considered. NPs typically have high molecular weight and poor solubility, which hinder their absorption and transport in the human body [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib10)]. However, they often possess numerous functional groups and multitarget binding capabilities, necessitating subsequent structural optimization to improve drug likeness and specificity [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib11)]. This may explain the relatively low proportion of drugs directly sourced from native NPs ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#fig1)B), as most drugs require modification and optimization (NI and ND in [Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#fig1)B) based on their original NP structures. The third challenge is the low abundance of most NPs in nature and the difficulty in synthesizing structurally complex products. NPs often exhibit complex ring systems and multiple chiral centers, making the total synthesis lengthy and inefficient. Biosynthesis is constrained by unknown pathways and corresponding enzymes. As shown in [Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#fig1)C, only 30% of the molecules in COCONUT are annotated with known biological sources, and less than 7% are annotated with complete biosynthetic pathways according to structure matching with metabolic databases, such as KEGG [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib12)] and MetaCyc [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib13)].

![Image 9: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2773216924000163-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (113KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2773216924000163-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (113KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2773216924000163-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig.1. The distribution of natural products under different classification criteria. (A) Distribution of active NPs (NPA) and NP drugs (NPD). (B) Distribution of categories of approved small molecule drugs according the data from Newman's work [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib1)]. S, Synthetic drugs; NI, natural products inspired drugs (corresponding to S∗ and NM of the original work); ND, natural products derived drugs; NP, natural products (corresponding to N and NB of the original work). (C) Distribution of biological sources of natural products. NPO, natural products with biological source annotations; NPS, natural products with biosynthetic pathway annotations.

With the advancement of big data and information technology, computation-enabled natural drug discovery has become increasingly significant, and NP databases play a key role in this regard. Both large-scale databases (such as COCONUT [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib8)] and SuperNatural [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib14)]) and specialized databases (such as activity-focused NPASS [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib9)], terpenoid-focused TeroMOL [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib15)], and the marine NP database CMNPD [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib16)]) continue to evolve and improve. Several reviews have categorized existing NP databases [[[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib18), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib19), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib20), [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib21), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib22)]; others have provided a systematic analysis of the chemical space of multiple databases [[[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib23), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib24), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib25)]. Various databases have been built continuously, and a considerable number of them are no longer updated or are even inaccessible [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib17)]. Therefore, rather than redundantly listing existing databases, this paper summarizes the typical features ([Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#fig2) and [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#tbl1)) and representative applications of NP databases and highlights their importance in order to accelerate drug discovery ([Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#fig3)). Additionally, we discuss the potential challenges and future prospects of NP databases with the primary aim of facilitating the development and maintenance of these databases, thereby better empowering natural drug discovery.

![Image 10: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2773216924000163-gr2.jpg)

Fig.3. Representative applications of natural products databases for drug discovery.

2. Key features of NP databases
-------------------------------

As the significance of NPs in drug discovery continues to grow, the design and functionality of NP databases are evolving. These databases are required to not only provide comprehensive structural information but also integrate multidimensional data, such as the properties, biological sources, and biosynthesis pathways of NPs. This integration aims to facilitate in-depth pharmaceutical research. Most databases use web platforms to provide users with information. This section focuses on the key features of NP databases that collectively constitute the core value of these repositories.

### 2.1. Structure data

One of the most crucial pieces of information in a molecular database is the molecular structure, which is paramount for drug screening purposes. Almost all databases store molecular structure information in the form of simplified molecular input line entry system (SMILES) [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib26)], commonly accompanied by an international chemical identifier (InChI) [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib27)]. These representations condense two-dimensional topological structure information into one-dimensional strings, rendering them widely used in databases. String-like forms make them suitable for natural language processing, which has recently been widely used in property prediction, molecular generation, and reaction prediction [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib28)]. Moreover, text files are frequently used for sharing libraries of three-dimensional structure data, such as SDF and MOL files, where a series of molecules can be joined together and annotated with additional information. These files can be directly used as molecular libraries for virtual screening and chemical similarity analyses [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib29)]. These structures can also be used as inputs for physical-based modeling such as quantum chemistry calculations and molecular dynamic simulations [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib30)]. The string representations and text files can be mutually converted through common cheminformatics tools such as RDKit [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib31)] and Open Bable [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib32)]. InChIKey [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib33)] serves as a hash representation of InChI, allowing for a one-way conversion from InChI to InChiKey without the possibility of reversal. Although InChiKey does not convey topological structure information, its fixed length of 25 characters makes it conducive for the swift matching and retrieval of structural information within databases.

In addition to textual representations, many online databases have adopted skeletal formula representations to enhance the visualization of molecular structures. For instance, the COCONUT database generates image formats of two-dimensional structures for each molecule, whereas SuperNatural 3.0 [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib14)] employs the ChemDoodle [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib34)] plugin, embedded in web pages for structure rendering. Spectral data, including nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and mass spectrometry (MS) data, offer crucial insights for structural identification and comparison, particularly for complex NPs. However, in contrast to the diversity of NP structural databases, only a few databases, including specialized databases such as GNPS [[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib35)], NP-MRD [[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib36)] and NMRdata [[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib37)] and the general-purpose database PubChem [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib38)], store spectral data.

### 2.2. Property annotations

The structural features and properties of compounds play pivotal roles in determining their pharmacological efficacy during drug development. Consequently, many databases have annotated compound-related features and properties. With advancements in machine learning, these pieces of information are commonly termed descriptors. Common descriptors include parameters associated with Lipinski's rule of five [[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib39)], such as the number of hydrogen bond donors/acceptors, rotatable bonds, and lipophilicity, as well as those related to ADMET properties such as blood-brain barrier permeability and hepatotoxicity [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib40)]. Cheminformatics tools such as RDKit can be used to calculate some of the descriptors, for example, Lipinski's rule of five. The prediction of ADMET properties is more challenging [[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib41)] and several computational tools have been developed using the web interface [[[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib42), [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib43), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib44)]. With the help of increasing data, computational approaches can achieve acceptable accuracy in predicting certain properties (such as hERG inhibitory activities) using 2D descriptors; however, not all endpoints are predicted accurately [[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib45)]. For example, the prediction of time-dependent CYP inhibition, which relies on information from 3D fragments, is difficult because of the lag compared to other datasets. Moreover, despite the rapid development of computational models, we are still far from being able to replace the experimental validation.

In addition to the aforementioned descriptors, another critical aspect directly relevant to drug discovery is the biological activity of the molecules. Original literature often elucidates the inhibitory, activating, or other relevant activities of compounds against specific targets, cells, or organisms. These activities are often associated with broader indications or functions, such as anticancer or antimicrobial effects, promoting the construction of knowledge graphs and contributing to activity prediction and drug repurposing. Databases collect activity data as annotations directly from literature sources. For instance, the NPASS [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib9)] currently encompasses over 950,000 activity records of approximately 100,000 compounds. NPACT [[46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib46)] encampasses anticancer active molecules from literature sources, along with their corresponding cancer cell lines and activity values. Some databases align their structures with general-purpose activity databases to extract and annotate the corresponding biological activities. For example, TeroKit [[47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib47)] and CMNPD [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib16)] integrate target names, types, and other information extracted from the ChEMBL database [[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib48)].

### 2.3. Biological sources

In NP databases, annotations of biological sources of compounds serve as crucial metadata and provide valuable contextual information for researchers. These annotations typically include details about the organisms from which the compounds were isolated, such as plant species, microbial strains, or marine organisms. In addition, they may encompass information regarding the geographic location of the source organism, ecological habitats, and collection methods employed for isolation. For example, the NuBBE [[49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib49)] database contains a variety of NPs isolated from Brazil and provides information on the species and their locations. NPcVar [[50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773216924000163#bib50)] is a database that describes the content variation of NPs, where the NP content data are associated with the species, parts of the organism, experimental details, and other factors. These annotations not only aid in understanding the biodiversity and ecological significance of NPs but also facilitate the discovery of novel bioactive molecules from untapped natural sources. Overall, the inclusion of comprehensive biological source annotations enhances the utility and interpretability of NP databases, thereby fostering advancements in NP research and drug discovery.

As a special category of NP databases, biological source information in traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) databases is more important and needs to be more detailed. These annotations encompass the medicinal herbs or herbal formulations from which the compounds are derived. They often include the botanical and traditional Chinese names of herbs as well as their corresponding English translations. Furthermore, annotations may provide insights into the historical and cultural significance of medicinal plants, including their traditional uses, preparation methods, and therapeutic indications in traditional Chinese medicine practice. enriched_points.expanded_content: Bioinformatics tools analyze large datasets to discover unique natural products, revealing bioactive compounds that could lead to innovative drug development opportunities.
By combining structural analysis with functional genomics, bioinformatics enhances understanding of natural product mechanisms, facilitating the identification of potential therapeutic targets.
Advancements in computational methods optimize lead compounds, augmenting their efficacy and safety, thus boosting the therapeutic potential of newly identified natural products.
